question: A manufacturer has observed that over the course of 10 months, the numbers of products demanded were $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{10}$, respectively. If we assume that these are i.i.d. observations of demand, what bound can be put on the probability that their average $ ar{T}= rac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} T_i}{10}$ is more than $3 \sigma$ products from the average demand $\mu$ ?
answer:
We obtain

$$
E( ar{T})=\mu, \quad \operatorname{Var}( ar{T})= rac{\sigma^2}{10}
$$


From the Chebyshev's inequality,

$$
P\{| ar{T}-\mu| \geq 3 \sigma\}=P\left\{\left| rac{ ar{T}-\mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{10}}ight| \geq 3 \sqrt{10}ight\} \leq  rac{1}{(3 \sqrt{10})^2}= rac{1}{90}
$$


Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{\mathrm{n}}$ and $X$ be random variables on a probability space. We say that $X_{\mathrm{n}}$ converges in probability to $X$ if
$$
 egin{aligned}
&\lim _{n ightarrow \infty} P\left(\left|X_n-Xight|<\varepsilonight)=1 \quad 	ext { for every } \varepsilon>0\
&	ext { Similarly, } X_{\mathrm{n}} 	ext { converges with probability } 1 	ext { (or almost surely) to } X 	ext { if }\
&P\left(\lim _{n ightarrow \infty} X_n=Xight)=1 .
\end{aligned}
$$
